title: The Aspirations Of HPE And Dell In The Quantum-Classical HPC Datacenter
gist: HPE and Dell are positioning themselves as integrators (not quantum hardware builders) treating QPUs as another accelerator alongside CPUs and GPUs in hybrid HPC datacenters, with HPE partnering across Intel, IQM, Quantinuum, QuEra, and Rigetti and Dell working with 20+ quantum companies including Equal1, whose rack-mountable, CMOS-fabricated RacQ silicon-spin quantum system was demoed alongside Dell PowerEdge servers; both vendors and their quantum partners argue enterprises are shifting from cloud-only quantum access toward on-premises quantum-classical co-location because 'the silicon is the cheap part' relative to qubits.

themes.theme: OEM strategy: integrator, not quantum builder

insights.claim: HPE's HPC and AI infrastructure SVP/GM Trish Damkroger says HPE will not build quantum devices itself, just as it doesn't design silicon, but instead positions QPUs as another accelerator that HPE integrates via networking, a unified control plane, and manufacturing built around Nvidia reference designs.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: HPE's business model in quantum mirrors its GPU-era role as systems integrator rather than chip designer, meaning HPE's quantum upside is tied to integration/services revenue and rack-scale manufacturing margins rather than quantum IP, similar to how it monetizes Nvidia GPU racks today.
insights.quote: We will not make the quantum devices, just like we don't make the silicon. I look at quantum as another accelerator. We will be putting the network to it. You've got to bring that all together.
insights.locator: For HPE, it's all about the integration point

insights.claim: Dell global CTO and chief AI officer John Roese states that no quantum computers exist that are built with QPUs alone; all are hybrid systems, and Dell's strategy is to be quantum vendors' integration partner, treating QPUs like GPUs so Dell customers can consume industrialized hybrid quantum systems.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Confirms Dell is chasing the same hybrid-integrator positioning as HPE rather than competing on quantum hardware itself, implying quantum computing vendors (Equal1, IQM, etc.) will likely need OEM partners like Dell/HPE to reach enterprise datacenters at scale, a channel dependency worth tracking for quantum hardware startups' go-to-market.
insights.quote: There are no quantum computers that are only built with QPUs. They are all hybrid quantum systems. Our strategy is to be their best friend, to treat it like an accelerator, just like GPUs.
insights.locator: Dell is following the same path.

insights.claim: HPE announced partnerships at its Discover 2026 event with Intel, IQM Quantum Computers, Quantinuum, QuEra, and Rigetti to integrate multiple quantum modalities with its Cray supercomputer family, including testbeds for hybrid algorithm design, software interoperability, and HPC-quantum workflow benchmarking.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Gives investors the current roster of quantum hardware vendors with confirmed OEM distribution/integration deals via HPE, a signal of which quantum modality players are gaining enterprise channel traction versus those without major OEM backing.
insights.quote: At its Discover 2026 show last month, HPE announced partnerships with Intel, IQM Quantum Computers, Quantinuum, QuEra, and Rigetti, among others, as the system maker builds out a platform for integrating quantum systems of multiple modalities with its family of Cray supercomputers.
insights.locator: At its Discover 2026 show last month

insights.claim: Dell has worked with more than 20 quantum computing companies over the past 10 years, according to John Roese, positioning it as a broad, modality-agnostic hybrid integration partner rather than betting on a single quantum technology.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A decade-long, 20+-company partner network suggests Dell's quantum integration effort is more mature and diversified than headline announcements alone would suggest, relevant to assessing Dell's readiness to capture quantum-classical infrastructure revenue as the market matures.
insights.quote: The vendor over the past 10 years has worked with more than 20 quantum companies, according to John Roese, global chief technology officer and chief AI officer at Dell.
insights.locator: Dell is following the same path.

insights.claim: Nvidia does not build quantum computers but supplies the software/library layer for hybrid classical-quantum computing via CUDA-Q (programming model), cuQuantum (circuit simulation libraries), and DGX-Quantum (error correction), partnering with rather than competing against quantum hardware vendors.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Nvidia is establishing a software/platform lock-in position across the quantum computing stack analogous to CUDA's role in GPU computing, positioning it to capture value from quantum adoption regardless of which hardware vendor wins, without quantum hardware capex risk.
insights.quote: We don't build quantum computers, and yet we are deeply integrated into the quantum computing industry and we create libraries. CUDA-Q is the programming model for hybrid classical accelerated quantum. We have cuQuantum... and DGX-Quantum, to do error correction in quantum computers.
insights.locator: Nvidia clearly sees a quantum future

insights.claim: HPE is now manufacturing rack-scale AI systems to Nvidia's reference designs rather than designing the racks itself, differentiating only around the edges, illustrating how much of the value chain Nvidia now controls even for hardware partners like HPE.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Underscores Nvidia's design-authority leverage over its OEM partners even outside quantum, a relevant baseline for judging how much independent differentiation HPE and Dell can realistically capture as quantum gets integrated into Nvidia-centric rack architectures.
insights.quote: If you look at rack-scale now, we're not even designing the rack... We are manufacturing it, but Nvidia is giving us a reference design, and then we add what we can to differentiate around that.
insights.locator: For HPE, it's all about the integration point

themes.theme: Shift from cloud-only to on-premises quantum

insights.claim: Damkroger says quantum computing access is shifting from pure cloud consumption (e.g., Oak Ridge National Laboratory ordering quantum time over the cloud) toward organizations wanting physical on-premises devices, and compares the current quantum market maturity to the era of the Cray-1 supercomputer's 1975 launch.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: The 'Cray-1 moment' framing implies quantum computing is at the very early innings of a decades-long on-premises hardware cycle analogous to supercomputing's own build-out, suggesting a long runway for infrastructure capex but also a long timeline before mainstream ROI.
insights.quote: We're not at the Cray-1 level yet, but we're getting there. We're going to start really seeing those Cray-1 quantum machines produced.

insights.claim: IQM has sold 23 on-premises quantum systems globally and is focused on on-premises datacenter deployment even while offering cloud access via its Resonance service; Oak Ridge National Laboratory's June 2026 'Pathfinder' installation of a 20-qubit IQM Radiance system was IQM's first US sale and the lab's first quantum system purchase.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of the cloud-to-on-premises transition already happening at a flagship US national lab, a leading indicator for enterprise/government quantum procurement patterns and a reference customer IQM can point to in future sales.
insights.quote: the Finland-based vendor is focused on on-premises datacenters, as shown by its having sold 23 systems around the world... Oak Ridge, whose "Frontier" supercomputer is built on HPE's Cray EX235A HPC systems, in June announced that Pathfinder, a 20-qubit IQM Radiance quantum system, went operational. It's the first quantum system bought by the national lab and the first IQM has sold in the United States.

insights.claim: IQM North America country director Ron Bewtra argues the most credible near-term commercial path for quantum computing runs through HPC integration rather than pure cloud access or standalone systems.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A vendor-side view worth weighing against pure-cloud quantum access models (e.g., IBM Quantum, AWS Braket); if HPC co-location is indeed the winning path, quantum vendors without HPC/datacenter integration partnerships may lose ground to those with HPE/Dell-style alliances.
insights.quote: There has been healthy debate about where quantum computing finds its first real foothold in enterprise... My view, shaped by what I've seen in the U.S. market, is that the most credible near-term path runs through HPC.

insights.claim: D-Wave, historically a pure cloud annealing-quantum provider via its Leap service, began selling its Advantage systems directly to organizations for on-premises deployment starting last year (2025).
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Another data point confirming an industry-wide business-model shift among quantum vendors from cloud-subscription-only toward direct hardware sales, relevant to modeling D-Wave's revenue mix transition.
insights.quote: D-Wave, which for most of its history has offered annealing quantum computing via its Leap cloud service, but the company began selling its Advantage systems directly to organizations last year.
insights.locator: D-Wave, which for most of its history

themes.theme: Equal1's RacQ: rack-mountable CMOS quantum chip

insights.claim: Equal1's RacQ quantum computer, demoed at Dell Technologies World 2026, fits in a standard 19-inch/42U datacenter rack, runs on about 1.6 kilowatts, weighs 400 kilograms, and uses an integrated cryocooler maintaining 0.3 Kelvin, eliminating the need for external cryogenic infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A self-contained, standard-rack-form-factor quantum system with modest power draw (1.6kW) is a concrete engineering milestone that could materially lower the facilities/infrastructure barrier to enterprise quantum adoption compared to systems requiring dedicated cryogenic plants.
insights.quote: The system runs at about 1.6 kilowatts, weight 400 kilograms – it can fit in a standard Dell 42U frame – and includes an integrated cryocooler that keeps an internal temperature of 0.3 Kelvin.
insights.locator: It's built using standard semiconductor processes

insights.claim: Equal1 fabricates its silicon-spin qubit quantum processor using standard CMOS semiconductor manufacturing processes, creating a quantum system-on-a-chip that integrates all system components into a single silicon package for easier scalability.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Using standard CMOS fabrication (versus exotic superconducting or trapped-ion processes) could let Equal1 leverage existing semiconductor foundry capacity and cost curves, a potential structural cost advantage over other quantum modalities if the approach scales.
insights.quote: Equal1's modality of choice is silicon-spin qubits that are fabricated using standard CMOS, creating a quantum system-on-a-chip (SOC) that puts all the components of the system into a single silicon package.
insights.locator: One of those companies is Equal1

insights.claim: Dell CTO John Roese says scaling Equal1's chip from 5 qubits to 1,000 or a million qubits requires shrinking the qubit-bearing semiconductor from about 3 square millimeters to 11 square millimeters within a 300-millikelvin environment, and converting the control system from discrete boxes into a Cryo-CMOS processor that runs compute logic inside the super-cooled environment itself.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Identifies the two specific engineering bottlenecks (qubit die scaling and Cryo-CMOS control integration) that gate Equal1's — and by extension similar silicon-spin approaches' — path to useful qubit counts, giving investors concrete technical milestones to track for scaling credibility.
insights.quote: For that Equal1 system to go from a five-qubit system to 1,000 qubits or even a million qubits, it requires two engineering feats... they have to take the control system, which is now a bunch of boxes, and turn it into a processor and then use what's called Cryo-CMOS.

insights.claim: Roese describes being able to place the Equal1 RacQ system directly next to an Nvidia NVL72 rack and connect the two, forming a combined CPU-GPU-QPU compute system using Dell servers as the classical hybrid layer.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A concrete demonstrated architecture (QPU co-located with Nvidia NVL72 GPU racks) shows how quantum accelerators could plug directly into existing AI datacenter infrastructure investments rather than requiring separate facilities, a relevant data point for how quantum capex may piggyback on the current GPU buildout.
insights.quote: I could roll that in right next to an Nvidia NVL72 rack and connect the two things together and I have all the basic compute to do a CPU-GPU-QPU system.
insights.locator: Roese added that with Dell servers

insights.claim: Dell's quantum infrastructure lead Burns Healy argues that relative to the cost and difficulty of building qubits, the classical silicon (CPU/GPU) side of a hybrid system is comparatively cheap, so real near-term quantum advantage requires co-locating QPUs with HPC rather than accessing them remotely via cloud.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Reframes the quantum computing cost equation: capital is best spent maximizing qubit throughput/accuracy via co-located classical compute (pre/post-processing, calibration, adaptive circuits) rather than treating classical compute as a bottleneck, informing how enterprises and investors should size HPC investment alongside quantum hardware purchases.
insights.quote: having a QPU in a lab that you can access via the cloud is neat, but if you want real kind of near-term quantum advantage, you're not going to have that not be co-located with HPC because, relative to qubits, the silicon is the cheap part.
insights.locator: Honestly, having a QPU in a lab

insights.claim: Healy says AI (predictive machine learning) will play a growing role in quantum-classical hybrid systems by helping generate quantum algorithms and by deciding in real time which processor type (GPU, QPU, CPU, or FPGA) is best suited to a given workload.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Positions AI-driven workload orchestration as a necessary layer for hybrid quantum-classical datacenters, suggesting a future software/orchestration market opportunity distinct from the quantum hardware itself, as different quantum modalities suit different workloads.
insights.quote: AI helps generate quantum algorithms, which will make quantum computing easier for the user... Predictive machine learning will help organizations with such needs as understanding when to turn on their GPU rather than their QPU, CPU, or FPGA.
insights.locator: AI will play a key role in this

insights.claim: Pre- and post-processing in hybrid quantum-classical computing stays on classical systems while only the exponentially-hard algorithmic subroutines run on the quantum processor, as demonstrated at Dell Technologies World 2026 using Dell PowerEdge servers alongside the Equal1 RacQ.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Clarifies the practical division of labor in hybrid quantum computing architectures, useful for investors assessing how much of the compute workload (and therefore infrastructure spend) in a 'quantum' system actually remains classical CPU/GPU spend.
insights.quote: Pre- and post-processing stays with the classical system; the quantum computer runs the quantum-intensive subroutines, or algorithmic building blocks the require mathematically heavy-duty computations exponentially faster than traditional systems.
insights.locator: The RacQ is part of what Equal1 calls
